did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Vision Transformer (ViT-L/16) model for melanoma classification from dermoscopic images. The study involved fine-tuning the ViT model on the ISIC 2019 dataset, generating synthetic images using StyleGAN2-ADA, and evaluating performance against CNN baselines and a commercial diagnostic tool (MoleAnalyzer Pro) on the MN187 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a multi-dataset evaluation study. It describes specific methods including fine-tuning a Vision Transformer (ViT-L/16) on the ISIC 2019 dataset, generating synthetic images with StyleGAN2-ADA, and evaluating performance on the MN187 dataset. It reports quantitative results (ROC-AUC scores, p-values from DeLong's test) and compares the proposed model against CNN baselines and a commercial tool, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Clinical Application of Vision Transformers for Melanoma Classification: A Multi-Dataset Evaluation Study
brief_summary: This study evaluated the clinical application of Vision Transformers for melanoma classification using the ISIC 2019 dataset and synthetic data generated by StyleGAN2-ADA. A ViT-L/16 model was fine-tuned on over 25,000 dermoscopic images and augmented with 46,000 high-confidence synthetic images to balance class representation. The model's performance was assessed on the external biopsy-confirmed MN187 dataset and compared against CNN baselines and the commercial MoleAnalyzer Pro system using ROC-AUC and DeLong’s test. The ViT-L/16 model achieved a baseline ROC-AUC of 0.902, which improved to 0.915 after GAN augmentation, surpassing all CNN baselines and the commercial tool. Although the initial improvement over the commercial system was not statistically significant (p = 0.07), the augmented model showed a statistically significant advantage (p = 0.032). These results suggest that Vision Transformers, particularly when combined with GAN-based augmentation, offer strong potential for dependable AI-driven clinical decision support in melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for classification. Included in ISIC 2019 training (4522 images) and validation (1327 images) sets, and MN187 test set (91 images). Also included in synthetic data generation.
Primary comparator class to melanoma. Included in ISIC 2019 training (12,875 images) and validation (2495 images) sets, and MN187 test set (96 images). Also included in synthetic data generation.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into melanoma (malignant) versus nevus (benign) categories.
all_task_types.details: The study performs binary classification to distinguish between malignant melanoma and benign nevi. The model outputs a probability score between 0 and 1 indicating the probability of malignancy, evaluated using ROC-AUC scores on the MN187 dataset. The task is explicitly described as 'binary classification (melanoma versus nevus)' in the conclusions.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A large and diverse collection of dermoscopic images compiled by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration, containing high-resolution images of skin lesions annotated by expert dermatologists.

use.training: Used for training and fine-tuning the ViT-L/16 model.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (original ISIC-2019 test set repurposed as validation set).
use.other: Used for validation of baseline CNN models and ViT-B/16.

datasets_used.name: MN187
datasets_used.description: An external biopsy-confirmed dataset of de-identified dermoscopic images obtained from the VA dermatology clinic (College of Medicine, University of South Florida). Images were initially analyzed by the FotoFinder AI MoleAnalyzer Pro.

use.testing: Used as the external test set to evaluate the model's generalization to unseen clinical data and compare performance against CNN baselines and MoleAnalyzer Pro.

datasets_used.name: Synthetic GAN-generated Images
datasets_used.description: High-confidence synthetic melanoma and nevus images produced using StyleGAN2-ADA to expand the dataset and balance class representation.

use.training: Added to the training set to augment the ISIC 2019 data and address class imbalance.
use.other: Used for data augmentation via GAN-based generation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the statistical significance of performance differences by comparing the ROC-AUC of each model against a predefined baseline model (MoleAnalyzer Pro).
To quantify the uncertainty associated with each AUC difference.
To determine statistical significance, where a p-value below 0.05 indicates a significant difference in model performance.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: No difference in ROC-AUC exists between the baseline model (MoleAnalyzer Pro) and the evaluated model (AUC_baseline = AUC_evaluated).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: A difference in ROC-AUC exists between the baseline model and the evaluated model (AUC_baseline ≠ AUC_evaluated).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A p-value below 0.05 was interpreted as evidence of a statistically significant difference in model performance, whereas values of p ≥ 0.05 were considered consistent with random variation, implying no significant difference between models.

baseline_experiment.notes: The ViT-L/16 model achieved the highest ROC-AUC (0.902) among all models, surpassing the commercial MoleAnalyzer Pro (0.856), but the difference was not statistically significant (p=0.07). None of the models showed a statistically significant improvement over the baseline at alpha=0.05.

augmented_experiment.notes: After adding 46,000 high-confidence synthetic images, the ViT-L/16 model's ROC-AUC increased to 0.915, achieving a statistically significant advantage over MoleAnalyzer Pro (p=0.032). A variant with fine-tuned last 6 blocks achieved 0.926 (p=0.006). All statistical comparisons were performed relative to MoleAnalyzer Pro.

baseline_experiment.notes: Validation results based on the repurposed ISIC-2019 test set. ConvNeXt-XL achieved the highest validation score.

augmented_experiment.notes: Validation results for models trained with augmented data (real ISIC-2019 + synthetic images).

preprocessing_techniques: Image normalization using ImageNet mean ([0.485, 0.456, 0.406]) and standard deviation ([0.229, 0.224, 0.225])
Random resized cropping to 224 pixels with a scale range of 0.7 to 1.0
GAN-based data augmentation using StyleGAN2-ADA to generate synthetic images
Input image resolution set to 256x256 for StyleGAN2-ADA training

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random resized cropping to 224 pixels with a scale range of 0.7 to 1.0; Random horizontal flipping.

StyleGAN2-ADA used to generate synthetic images for both melanoma and nevus classes. Separate GAN models were trained for each class on images resized to 256x256 pixels. Each GAN produced 25,000 images (total 50,000). Training used 2 NVIDIA RTX A5000 GPUs, Adam optimizer, learning rate 0.002-0.0025, batch size 16, and 1000 kimg (1M samples). Early stopping criterion was FID < 50.

Generated synthetic images were filtered using the ViT-L/16 classifier to retain only high-confidence samples. Thresholds: probability > 0.6 for melanoma and < 0.4 for nevus. This resulted in 27,793 synthetic nevus and 18,438 synthetic melanoma images being added to the training set.

GAN augmentation was used to address class imbalance in the ISIC 2019 dataset (originally 74.0% nevus, 26.0% melanoma). The augmented dataset adjusted class proportions to 63.9% nevus and 36.1% melanoma.

conclusions: Vision Transformers, particularly the ViT-L/16 architecture, show strong potential for melanoma classification, offering advantages in global feature representation compared to CNN baselines.
Combining Vision Transformers with GAN-based data augmentation (specifically StyleGAN2-ADA) significantly improves model performance and helps address class imbalance in training datasets.

The ViT-L/16 model achieved a baseline ROC-AUC of 0.902 on the external MN187 dataset, surpassing all CNN baselines and the commercial MoleAnalyzer Pro system, although this initial difference was not statistically significant (p = 0.07).

After augmentation with 46,000 high-confidence synthetic images, the ViT-L/16 model's ROC-AUC increased to 0.915, achieving a statistically significant improvement over the MoleAnalyzer Pro system (p = 0.032).

A refined ViT-L/16 variant, created by fine-tuning the final six transformer layers, achieved an ROC-AUC of 0.926, which was also statistically significantly better than the commercial benchmark (p = 0.006).

The study demonstrates that limited, targeted adaptation of high-level self-attention layers can align global contextual features with dermoscopic lesion morphology without causing instability or overfitting.
The models maintained generalization capabilities when evaluated on the external biopsy-confirmed MN187 dataset, performing at a level similar to or exceeding the commercial MoleAnalyzer Pro.
The approach supports the development of dependable AI-driven clinical decision-support systems for melanoma detection, potentially helping doctors make faster and more confident assessments.
important_remarks: The external validation dataset (MN187) was small (n=187) and sourced from a single institution (VA dermatology clinic), limiting generalizability and increasing the risk of population bias.
The study focused exclusively on binary classification (melanoma vs. nevus), ignoring other skin lesion categories, which limits the clinical relevance for broader dermatological practice.

The baseline ViT-L/16 model did not show a statistically significant improvement over the commercial MoleAnalyzer Pro (p=0.07); statistical significance was only achieved after GAN augmentation (p=0.032).
The MN187 dataset images were initially classified by the commercial tool (MoleAnalyzer Pro) before biopsy confirmation, which may introduce bias in the ground truth or evaluation process.

GAN-generated synthetic images were filtered using the model's own predictions (high-confidence sampling), which poses a risk of data leakage or circular reasoning if the filter is not independent of the training process.
The study acknowledges that while the approach shows potential, real-world clinical deployment requires larger, more diverse datasets and integration of clinical metadata to be practically relevant.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the ISIC 2019 dataset for training and validation, with the original test set repurposed as the validation set. Synthetic data generated via StyleGAN2-ADA was added to the training set to address class imbalance. The MN187 dataset, an external biopsy-confirmed cohort from a single VA clinic, was used for final testing to evaluate generalization. The study performed cross-dataset validation by training on ISIC 2019 (with synthetic augmentation) and testing on the unseen MN187 dataset.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The model was trained on the ISIC 2019 dataset (augmented with synthetic images) and tested on the completely separate, unseen MN187 dataset. This constitutes external validation.

training.notes: Used for initial training and fine-tuning of the ViT-L/16 and CNN baselines.
Generated by StyleGAN2-ADA and filtered by high-confidence predictions from the baseline ViT-L/16 model. Added to the ISIC 2019 training set to balance classes and expand data volume.

validation.notes: The original ISIC-2019 test set was repurposed as the validation set for model selection and hyperparameter tuning during the training process.

testing.notes: Used as the held-out external test set to evaluate final model performance and generalization to unseen clinical data.

external_testing.notes: External biopsy-confirmed dataset from the VA dermatology clinic (University of South Florida). Used to assess generalizability beyond the training distribution.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: Pretrained weights were used to initialize the Vision Transformer backbone (ViT-L/16) and CNN backbones (ResNet-152, DenseNet-201, etc.) before fine-tuning on the dermoscopic datasets.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Synthetic images were generated for both melanoma and nevus classes. High-confidence samples (probability > 0.6 for melanoma, < 0.4 for nevus) were selected and added to the training set. This served as data augmentation to address class imbalance.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: ISIC 2019: 17,397 training, 3,822 validation. MN187: 187 test images. No random split percentages reported for MN187 as it was a fixed external cohort.
evaluation_protocol.cross_validation_details: Five independent training runs were performed for each model configuration (baseline and augmented) on the ISIC 2019 training set, with performance averaged over these runs for validation and testing metrics.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: The MN187 dataset was the unseen external data source, distinct from the ISIC 2019 training/validation distribution.

dataset_level_results.notes: Statistically significant improvement over MoleAnalyzer Pro (p=0.032). A variant with fine-tuned last 6 blocks achieved 0.926 (p=0.006).
Did not achieve statistical significance over MoleAnalyzer Pro (p=0.07).

cross_dataset_experiments.notes: All models were trained on ISIC 2019 (with/without augmentation) and tested on the external MN187 dataset. This represents a true cross-dataset validation experiment.

generalizability_notes: The external validation dataset (MN187) was small (n=187) and sourced from a single institution (VA dermatology clinic), limiting generalizability and increasing the risk of population bias.
The study focused exclusively on binary classification (melanoma vs. nevus), ignoring other skin lesion categories, which limits the clinical relevance for broader dermatological practice.
The MN187 dataset images were initially classified by the commercial tool (MoleAnalyzer Pro) before biopsy confirmation, which may introduce bias in the ground truth or evaluation process.

GAN-generated synthetic images were filtered using the model's own predictions (high-confidence sampling), which poses a risk of data leakage or circular reasoning if the filter is not independent of the training process.
information_not_reported: Specific patient-level separation details for MN187 (whether images from the same patient were split across train/test).
Detailed demographic information for the MN187 cohort beyond biopsy confirmation status.
Sensitivity and specificity values for the models on the MN187 dataset (only ROC-AUC and p-values were reported).
